entries.title: How Endava is redesigning software delivery around AI agents
entries.summary: Learn how Endava is using AI agents, ChatGPT Enterprise, and Codex to accelerate software delivery, automate workflows, and build an AI-native culture across the enterprise.

entries.title: So You Have an AI Security Budget. Now what?
entries.summary: An AI security budget should fund more than visibility. The real priority is unified governance and enforcement across agentic development and production apps.

entries.title: Node-gyp Supply Chain Compromise: A Self-Propagating npm Worm That Hides in binding.gyp
entries.summary: A new npm worm is abusing binding.gyp to trigger node-gyp during install, letting malicious packages run code without lifecycle scripts. It steals credentials, persists in GitHub, and self-propagates across maintainers.

entries.title: Type Level Security: The future of secure AI code generation?
entries.summary: Secure-by-design types can turn common bugs into compile-time errors. This post explores how type-level security could help prevent entire classes of AI-generated vulnerabilities.

entries.title: The sorry state of skill distribution
entries.summary: Public skill marketplaces are being flooded with malicious skills that steal credentials, exfiltrate data, and hijack agents. In response, a segment of the security industry released skill scanners, a new family of tools designed to detect malicious skills before they’re installed. But we tested them, and they don’t work. We recently bypassed ClawHub’s malicious skill detector , Cisco’s agent skil

entries.title: OpenAI public policy agenda
entries.summary: OpenAI outlines its public policy agenda for AI, including safety, youth protection, workforce transition, and global standards to ensure AI benefits society.

entries.title: A blueprint for democratic governance of frontier AI
entries.summary: OpenAI outlines a blueprint for U.S. governance of frontier AI, proposing a federal framework for safety, resilience, and national security.

entries.title: Top 5 Tenable Nessus alternatives in 2026
entries.summary: Tenable Nessus is a powerful scanner, but powerful tools that nobody uses don't make software more secure. Compare five alternatives built for how engineering teams actually work. Category: DevSec Tools & Comparisons

entries.title: The New Security Risks of the Agentic Development Lifecycle
entries.summary: AI agents are changing how software gets built, and with it, where security risk begins. Learn why securing the process matters as much as securing the code.

entries.title: The npm Threat Landscape: Attack Surface and Mitigations (Updated June 2)
entries.summary: Unit 42 analyzes npm supply chain evolution post-Shai Hulud. Discover wormable malware, CI/CD persistence, multi-stage attacks and more. The post The npm Threat Landscape: Attack Surface and Mitigations (Updated June 2) appeared first on Unit 42 .

entries.title: Operation FlutterBridge: macOS Malvertising Campaign Spreads New FlutterShell Backdoor
entries.summary: Operation FlutterBridge is a malvertising campaign targeting macOS users. It distributed the new backdoor FlutterShell, built using the Flutter framework. The post Operation FlutterBridge: macOS Malvertising Campaign Spreads New FlutterShell Backdoor appeared first on Unit 42 .

entries.title: Protestware by open source maintainer to hinder agentic coding: The jqwik 1.10.0 Prompt Injection
entries.summary: jqwik 1.10.0 added a hidden prompt injection aimed at AI coding agents, using terminal escape codes to conceal destructive instructions from humans while leaving them readable to logs and tools.

entries.title: Trojanized ai-sdk-ollama Delivers Miasma, a Self-Replicating npm Worm via binding.gyp | Blog | Endor Labs
entries.summary: Endor Labs detected ai-sdk-ollama as part of an active self-replicating npm worm named ‘Miasma’ that uses binding.gyp to execute on install, steal cloud credentials, and spread itself.

entries.title: 不写代码，照样赢！全国首届文科生AI黑客松圆满落幕
Why EDR and proxy won’t save you from supply chain malware
entries.summary: EDR and proxies weren't built for supply chain malware. When malicious code arrives through npm install, it looks like normal behavior. Here's why that matters. Category: News
